title: Quiz: Value at Risk and Conditional Value at Risk
description: Test your understanding of VaR and CVaR risk measures

questions.question: What does a 95% 1-day VaR of $1 million mean?

options.text: The expected daily loss is $1 million
There is a 5% chance of losing more than $1 million in one day
The maximum possible loss is $1 million
95% of the portfolio value is $1 million

questions.explanation: VaR at 95% confidence means there is a 5% probability (1 - 95%) that losses will exceed the VaR amount over the specified time period.

questions.question: Which VaR method assumes returns follow a normal distribution?

options.text: Historical VaR
Monte Carlo VaR
Parametric VaR
Conditional VaR

questions.explanation: Parametric VaR (also called variance-covariance VaR) assumes returns follow a normal distribution and uses analytical formulas based on mean and standard deviation.

questions.question: What is Conditional Value at Risk (CVaR)?

options.text: VaR calculated under specific conditions
The expected loss given that loss exceeds VaR
VaR adjusted for correlation
The maximum possible loss

questions.explanation: CVaR (Expected Shortfall) is the expected loss in the worst cases - specifically, the average loss given that we're in the tail beyond VaR.

questions.question: Why is CVaR considered superior to VaR as a risk measure?

options.text: CVaR is easier to calculate
CVaR is a coherent risk measure (sub-additive)
CVaR always gives smaller values
CVaR doesn't require historical data

questions.explanation: CVaR is a coherent risk measure, meaning it always satisfies sub-additivity (diversification reduces risk). VaR can fail sub-additivity in some cases.

questions.question: CVaR is always greater than or equal to VaR at the same confidence level.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. CVaR is the average of losses beyond VaR, so it must be at least as large as the VaR threshold itself. CVaR ≥ VaR always.

questions.question: What is the main limitation of parametric VaR?

options.text: It's too slow to calculate
It underestimates tail risk due to normality assumption
It requires too much historical data
It can only be used for single assets

questions.explanation: Parametric VaR assumes normal returns, but real financial returns have fat tails (more extreme events than normal). This causes underestimation of tail risk.

questions.question: How can quantum computing potentially accelerate VaR estimation?

options.text: By using quantum entanglement to predict markets
By replacing Monte Carlo with Quantum Amplitude Estimation
By storing more historical data
Quantum computing cannot help with VaR

questions.explanation: Quantum Amplitude Estimation can estimate probabilities with O(1/ε) queries instead of O(1/ε²) for classical Monte Carlo, offering quadratic speedup.

questions.question: Which statements about VaR calculation methods are correct? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Historical VaR makes no distributional assumptions
Monte Carlo VaR can incorporate complex dependencies
Parametric VaR is always the most accurate
Historical VaR is limited by available data history

questions.explanation: Historical VaR is distribution-free but limited by data. Monte Carlo is flexible for complex models. Parametric is fast but not necessarily most accurate due to normality assumption.
